aiTitle: Generate phase 2 plan from workspace redesign docs

content.content:
1	# Phase 1 — workspace redesign storage tier
2	
3	Implements Phase 1 of the workspace redesign per `docs/plans/2026-05-01-workspace-redesign-design.md` and the slice plan at `docs/plans/2026-05-01-workspace-redesign-phase-1-plan.md`. Adds a sharded, container-shipped, native-`git`-binary storage tier (`@ax/workspace-git-server`) that speaks standard git smart-HTTP plus a tiny REST surface for repo lifecycle. Production keeps using `@ax/workspace-git-http` — the new tier is gated behind `gitServer.experimental.gitProtocol: false` for the canary phase. Phase 2 wires the host plugin and closes the half-wired window.
4	
5	## Summary
6	
7	- New package `@ax/workspace-git-server` (server + a test-only host-side library + integration tests).
8	- New container image at `container/git-server/Dockerfile` (debian-slim base, pinned git via apt, non-root, direct-execed Node).
9	- Helm chart additions (gated by `experimental.gitProtocol`): StatefulSet with per-shard PVCs via `volumeClaimTemplates`, headless Service for stable per-pod DNS, parallel NetworkPolicy permitting host pods only.
10	- Sharding by `(workspaceId)` — SHA-256 first 4 bytes mod N, no new dep, deterministic across host replicas.
11	- Argv-injection gate: `^[a-z0-9][a-z0-9_-]{0,62}$` workspaceId regex + `path.resolve` startsWith defense-in-depth + paranoid `git` env (full env replacement, never inheriting `process.env`).
12	- `runWorkspaceContract` reuse — all 9 contract assertions pass against the new tier.
13	- Multi-replica concurrency test, empty-repo materialize test, 35-input argv-injection acceptance test (× 6 routes = 222 assertions).
14	
15	393 tests in `@ax/workspace-git-server`, 6 in `@ax/chart-tests`, all green.
16	
17	## Open question resolutions (per the plan)
18	
19	| # | Question | Resolution |
20	|---|---|---|
21	| 1 | HA per shard | Single-replica per shard for MVP; document as known limit. Recovery time on pod restart is "seconds." HA deferred to a separate design. |
22	| 2 | Re-sharding | Operator picks `gitServer.shards` at install time; changing later requires manual workspace migration (drain → rsync → flip traffic). Operational follow-up, not architectural. |
23	| 7 | First-time materialize | `POST /repos` creates an empty bare repo (no synthetic initial commit). Host treats "no `refs/heads/main`" as the empty-baseline case. First push from the host creates `main` atomically. v1's tempdir-bootstrap dance avoided. Validated by `__tests__/integration/empty-repo-materialize.test.ts`. |
24	| 4 | Container shape | Separate `ax-next/git-server` image. Host/runner stay slim; only the storage tier ships the git binary. |
25	| 5 | Shard hash | SHA-256 first 4 bytes mod N. No new dep. |
26	| 6 | WorkspaceId regex | `^[a-z0-9][a-z0-9_-]{0,62}$` (lowercase only, no dots, max 63 chars / DNS-label cap). Tighter than v1's. |
27	
28	## Cross-phase deviation: Option D adopted
29	
30	The original phasing said "replace storage, keep host's iso-git client for now." But `@ax/workspace-git-http` (JSON-over-HTTP) and the new server (git smart-HTTP) are not wire-compatible. Resolution: ship the server PLUS a **test-only** host-side adapter that satisfies `runWorkspaceContract`. Production keeps using the legacy stack until Phase 2 promotes the adapter into a registered plugin. See plan §"Cross-phase dependency surfaced" for the analysis.
31	
32	## Boundary review (per CLAUDE.md)
33	
34	The four canonical `workspace:*` hooks are unchanged by Phase 1 — the new server is reached over the wire from a future host plugin, not directly from the bus. The new wire surface (REST + git smart-HTTP) still gets the review:
35	
36	- **Alternate impl this wire could have:** Yes — Gitea, GitHub Enterprise, GitLab self-hosted. The wire is standard git smart-HTTP plus a tiny REST CRUD; the REST CRUD shape is the only thing each backend's adapter would translate (~50–200 LOC per backend).
37	- **Payload field names that might leak:** `headOid` and `default_branch: "main"` are git-shaped — but exposed only on the server's REST API (which is by definition git-shaped). The bus-level `workspace:*` hooks in `@ax/workspace-protocol` still use opaque `WorkspaceVersion` and don't expose `oid`. The leak stops at the storage-tier wire and never reaches subscribers.
38	- **Subscriber risk:** N/A in Phase 1 (no new subscribers; no plugin registers anything for this server in this PR — that's Phase 2).
39	- **Wire surface:** REST schemas live in `packages/workspace-git-server/src/server/repos.ts` (Zod, co-located with handlers). Smart-HTTP routes in `src/server/smart-http.ts`. NOT in `@ax/workspace-protocol` — the new wire is decoupled from the legacy JSON-over-HTTP wire.
40	
41	## Security review
42	
43	```
44	- Sandbox: New @ax/workspace-git-server container introduces process-spawn capability scoped to the literal command 'git' with fixed argv shape. Caller never controls argv0 or flags; the only caller-derived element is repoPath, built from a regex-validated workspaceId via path.join(repoRoot, ...) with a defense-in-depth path.resolve startsWith check. Locked-down env via PARANOID_GIT_ENV (full replacement, never { ...process.env, ... }). NetworkPolicy permits only inbound from host pods on the configured port; egress: []. Filesystem access bound to <repoRoot>/. SecurityContext: non-root UID 1000, all caps dropped, readOnlyRootFilesystem: true. Per-package SECURITY.md walks the budget in detail.
45	- Injection: Storage tier handles only opaque pack bytes (piped through git's stdin/stdout), regex-validated workspaceIds, bearer tokens compared via crypto.timingSafeEqual, and a 1 MiB JSON request body schema-validated by Zod. Bearer token never appears in any error message. Logged workspaceIds are regex-restricted (no newlines, control chars, ANSI). Model-output / tool-output / commit-message attack surface lives in Phase 3 (sandbox-side commit construction) — Phase 1's storage tier never sees agent-originated strings directly.
46	- Supply chain: Zero new npm runtime dependencies (uses Node stdlib + zod, both already in workspace). New runtime dependency: the git binary itself, pinned via container/git-server/Dockerfile to a specific apt version on debian-slim. SECURITY.md commits to monthly + critical-CVE rebase cadence. CVE-2024-32002 (clone-side RCE) and CVE-2024-32004 (clone-from-untrusted RCE) both N/A — the server only serves, never clones from untrusted sources; defense-in-depth protocol.allow=never set per-repo regardless.
47	```
48	
49	## Half-wired window — OPEN
50	
51	- New plugin loaded by: NONE (Phase 1 ships server + test-only client; no registered plugin)
52	- Test/canary that reaches it: `__tests__/contract.test.ts` (9 contract assertions), `__tests__/integration/multi-replica-concurrency.test.ts`, `__tests__/integration/empty-repo-materialize.test.ts`, `__tests__/integration/argv-injection.test.ts`
53	- User-facing surface: `gitServer.experimental.gitProtocol` chart toggle (operator-flippable; default off)
54	- Window CLOSES in: Phase 2 (replace registered host plugin with git-protocol client; promote `src/client/plugin-test-only.ts` to a real plugin or extract `@ax/workspace-git-server-client`)
55	- Successor PR: to be opened next
56	
57	## Migration & rollback
58	
59	**Rollback (post-deploy canary surfacing a problem):** flip `gitServer.experimental.gitProtocol: false`, `helm upgrade --reuse-values`. The new StatefulSet + headless Service + experimental NetworkPolicy un-render. Per-shard PVCs persist (`helm.sh/resource-policy: keep`) so bare repos survive for forensics. Production keeps running on the legacy `@ax/workspace-git-http` path the whole time — there's no traffic switchover to undo (Phase 1 doesn't wire any).
60	
61	Operator runbook: `docs/runbooks/2026-05-01-workspace-git-server-canary.md`.
62	
63	## Test plan
64	
65	- [x] `pnpm build` exit 0 across the monorepo.
66	- [x] `pnpm --filter @ax/workspace-git-server test` — 393 tests, all green.
67	- [x] `pnpm --filter @ax/chart-tests test` — 6 render assertions, all green (helm CLI required on PATH).
68	- [x] `docker build -f container/git-server/Dockerfile -t ax-next/git-server:dev .` succeeds; smoke test (`docker run` + `curl /healthz`) returns 200.
69	- [x] Contract test: 9/9 assertions pass against the new server path via the test-only adapter.
70	- [x] Multi-replica concurrency: two plugins racing on `parent: v0` → exactly one wins, loser retries on `parent: v1` → both changes land in linear history.
71	- [x] Empty-repo materialize: `POST /repos` creates empty bare repo; `git ls-remote` returns empty; first apply with `parent: null` lands main; subsequent fetch succeeds.
72	- [x] Argv-injection: 35 malicious inputs × 6 routes (REST + smart-HTTP) → all rejected before any spawn.
73	- [x] Helm render with toggle off → only legacy resources render.
74	- [x] Helm render with toggle on → STS + headless Service + experimental NP render alongside legacy resources.
75	- [x] Helm render with `gitServer.shards=3` → STS `replicas: 3`.
76	
77	🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
78

file.content:
# Phase 1 — workspace redesign storage tier

Implements Phase 1 of the workspace redesign per `docs/plans/2026-05-01-workspace-redesign-design.md` and the slice plan at `docs/plans/2026-05-01-workspace-redesign-phase-1-plan.md`. Adds a sharded, container-shipped, native-`git`-binary storage tier (`@ax/workspace-git-server`) that speaks standard git smart-HTTP plus a tiny REST surface for repo lifecycle. Production keeps using `@ax/workspace-git-http` — the new tier is gated behind `gitServer.experimental.gitProtocol: false` for the canary phase. Phase 2 wires the host plugin and closes the half-wired window.

## Summary

- New package `@ax/workspace-git-server` (server + a test-only host-side library + integration tests).
- New container image at `container/git-server/Dockerfile` (debian-slim base, pinned git via apt, non-root, direct-execed Node).
- Helm chart additions (gated by `experimental.gitProtocol`): StatefulSet with per-shard PVCs via `volumeClaimTemplates`, headless Service for stable per-pod DNS, parallel NetworkPolicy permitting host pods only.
- Sharding by `(workspaceId)` — SHA-256 first 4 bytes mod N, no new dep, deterministic across host replicas.
- Argv-injection gate: `^[a-z0-9][a-z0-9_-]{0,62}$` workspaceId regex + `path.resolve` startsWith defense-in-depth + paranoid `git` env (full env replacement, never inheriting `process.env`).
- `runWorkspaceContract` reuse — all 9 contract assertions pass against the new tier.
- Multi-replica concurrency test, empty-repo materialize test, 35-input argv-injection acceptance test (× 6 routes = 222 assertions).

393 tests in `@ax/workspace-git-server`, 6 in `@ax/chart-tests`, all green.

## Open question resolutions (per the plan)

| # | Question | Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | HA per shard | Single-replica per shard for MVP; document as known limit. Recovery time on pod restart is "seconds." HA deferred to a separate design. |
| 2 | Re-sharding | Operator picks `gitServer.shards` at install time; changing later requires manual workspace migration (drain → rsync → flip traffic). Operational follow-up, not architectural. |
| 7 | First-time materialize | `POST /repos` creates an empty bare repo (no synthetic initial commit). Host treats "no `refs/heads/main`" as the empty-baseline case. First push from the host creates `main` atomically. v1's tempdir-bootstrap dance avoided. Validated by `__tests__/integration/empty-repo-materialize.test.ts`. |
| 4 | Container shape | Separate `ax-next/git-server` image. Host/runner stay slim; only the storage tier ships the git binary. |
| 5 | Shard hash | SHA-256 first 4 bytes mod N. No new dep. |
| 6 | WorkspaceId regex | `^[a-z0-9][a-z0-9_-]{0,62}$` (lowercase only, no dots, max 63 chars / DNS-label cap). Tighter than v1's. |

## Cross-phase deviation: Option D adopted

The original phasing said "replace storage, keep host's iso-git client for now." But `@ax/workspace-git-http` (JSON-over-HTTP) and the new server (git smart-HTTP) are not wire-compatible. Resolution: ship the server PLUS a **test-only** host-side adapter that satisfies `runWorkspaceContract`. Production keeps using the legacy stack until Phase 2 promotes the adapter into a registered plugin. See plan §"Cross-phase dependency surfaced" for the analysis.

## Boundary review (per CLAUDE.md)

The four canonical `workspace:*` hooks are unchanged by Phase 1 — the new server is reached over the wire from a future host plugin, not directly from the bus. The new wire surface (REST + git smart-HTTP) still gets the review:

- **Alternate impl this wire could have:** Yes — Gitea, GitHub Enterprise, GitLab self-hosted. The wire is standard git smart-HTTP plus a tiny REST CRUD; the REST CRUD shape is the only thing each backend's adapter would translate (~50–200 LOC per backend).
- **Payload field names that might leak:** `headOid` and `default_branch: "main"` are git-shaped — but exposed only on the server's REST API (which is by definition git-shaped). The bus-level `workspace:*` hooks in `@ax/workspace-protocol` still use opaque `WorkspaceVersion` and don't expose `oid`. The leak stops at the storage-tier wire and never reaches subscribers.
- **Subscriber risk:** N/A in Phase 1 (no new subscribers; no plugin registers anything for this server in this PR — that's Phase 2).
- **Wire surface:** REST schemas live in `packages/workspace-git-server/src/server/repos.ts` (Zod, co-located with handlers). Smart-HTTP routes in `src/server/smart-http.ts`. NOT in `@ax/workspace-protocol` — the new wire is decoupled from the legacy JSON-over-HTTP wire.

## Security review

```
- Sandbox: New @ax/workspace-git-server container introduces process-spawn capability scoped to the literal command 'git' with fixed argv shape. Caller never controls argv0 or flags; the only caller-derived element is repoPath, built from a regex-validated workspaceId via path.join(repoRoot, ...) with a defense-in-depth path.resolve startsWith check. Locked-down env via PARANOID_GIT_ENV (full replacement, never { ...process.env, ... }). NetworkPolicy permits only inbound from host pods on the configured port; egress: []. Filesystem access bound to <repoRoot>/. SecurityContext: non-root UID 1000, all caps dropped, readOnlyRootFilesystem: true. Per-package SECURITY.md walks the budget in detail.
- Injection: Storage tier handles only opaque pack bytes (piped through git's stdin/stdout), regex-validated workspaceIds, bearer tokens compared via crypto.timingSafeEqual, and a 1 MiB JSON request body schema-validated by Zod. Bearer token never appears in any error message. Logged workspaceIds are regex-restricted (no newlines, control chars, ANSI). Model-output / tool-output / commit-message attack surface lives in Phase 3 (sandbox-side commit construction) — Phase 1's storage tier never sees agent-originated strings directly.
- Supply chain: Zero new npm runtime dependencies (uses Node stdlib + zod, both already in workspace). New runtime dependency: the git binary itself, pinned via container/git-server/Dockerfile to a specific apt version on debian-slim. SECURITY.md commits to monthly + critical-CVE rebase cadence. CVE-2024-32002 (clone-side RCE) and CVE-2024-32004 (clone-from-untrusted RCE) both N/A — the server only serves, never clones from untrusted sources; defense-in-depth protocol.allow=never set per-repo regardless.
```

## Half-wired window — OPEN

- New plugin loaded by: NONE (Phase 1 ships server + test-only client; no registered plugin)
- Test/canary that reaches it: `__tests__/contract.test.ts` (9 contract assertions), `__tests__/integration/multi-replica-concurrency.test.ts`, `__tests__/integration/empty-repo-materialize.test.ts`, `__tests__/integration/argv-injection.test.ts`
- User-facing surface: `gitServer.experimental.gitProtocol` chart toggle (operator-flippable; default off)
- Window CLOSES in: Phase 2 (replace registered host plugin with git-protocol client; promote `src/client/plugin-test-only.ts` to a real plugin or extract `@ax/workspace-git-server-client`)
- Successor PR: to be opened next

## Migration & rollback

**Rollback (post-deploy canary surfacing a problem):** flip `gitServer.experimental.gitProtocol: false`, `helm upgrade --reuse-values`. The new StatefulSet + headless Service + experimental NetworkPolicy un-render. Per-shard PVCs persist (`helm.sh/resource-policy: keep`) so bare repos survive for forensics. Production keeps running on the legacy `@ax/workspace-git-http` path the whole time — there's no traffic switchover to undo (Phase 1 doesn't wire any).

Operator runbook: `docs/runbooks/2026-05-01-workspace-git-server-canary.md`.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm build` exit 0 across the monorepo.
- [x] `pnpm --filter @ax/workspace-git-server test` — 393 tests, all green.
- [x] `pnpm --filter @ax/chart-tests test` — 6 render assertions, all green (helm CLI required on PATH).
- [x] `docker build -f container/git-server/Dockerfile -t ax-next/git-server:dev .` succeeds; smoke test (`docker run` + `curl /healthz`) returns 200.
- [x] Contract test: 9/9 assertions pass against the new server path via the test-only adapter.
- [x] Multi-replica concurrency: two plugins racing on `parent: v0` → exactly one wins, loser retries on `parent: v1` → both changes land in linear history.
- [x] Empty-repo materialize: `POST /repos` creates empty bare repo; `git ls-remote` returns empty; first apply with `parent: null` lands main; subsequent fetch succeeds.
- [x] Argv-injection: 35 malicious inputs × 6 routes (REST + smart-HTTP) → all rejected before any spawn.
- [x] Helm render with toggle off → only legacy resources render.
- [x] Helm render with toggle on → STS + headless Service + experimental NP render alongside legacy resources.
- [x] Helm render with `gitServer.shards=3` → STS `replicas: 3`.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

content.content:
365	    }),
366	  );
367	
368	  // ----- 3. workspace ----------------------------------------------------
369	  // Discriminated on `backend`. The shape difference is intentional: `local`
370	  // wants a filesystem path, `http` wants a URL + bearer token. Both register
371	  // the same four `workspace:*` service hooks (Invariant I1 — the contract is
372	  // the same regardless of backend).
373	  if (config.workspace.backend === 'http') {
374	    plugins.push(
375	      createWorkspaceGitHttpPlugin({
376	        baseUrl: config.workspace.baseUrl,
377	        token: config.workspace.token,
378	      }),
379	    );
380	  } else {
381	    plugins.push(
382	      createWorkspaceGitPlugin({
383	        repoRoot: config.workspace.repoRoot,
384	      }),
385	    );
386	  }
387	
388	  // ----- 4. audit log ----------------------------------------------------
389	  // Subscribes to event.http-egress and writes one row per egress via
390	  // storage:set. Push order isn't load-bearing — the kernel finishes init
391	  // (and therefore wires every subscriber) before any hook fires — but we
392	  // keep audit-log near the storage layer because that's its only call.
393	  plugins.push(auditLogPlugin());
394	
395	  // ----- 5. control-plane access (Week 9.5) -----------------------------
396	  // http-server provides the public-facing listener. auth registers the
397	  // /auth/* + /admin/{me,sign-out} routes and the auth:require-user gate
398	  // every admin endpoint depends on. teams owns /admin/teams* and the
399	  // teams:* hooks the agents-plugin's team-visibility ACL calls.
400	  //
401	  // Boot order: the kernel topologically sorts on `manifest.calls`
402	  // (http:register-route → @ax/http-server, auth:require-user → @ax/auth),
403	  // so even if we shuffled the array the kernel would init http-server
404	  // before auth before agents/teams. We push in the conceptual order to
405	  // keep the intent legible to readers.
406	  plugins.push(
407	    createHttpServerPlugin({
408	      host: config.http.host,
409	      port: config.http.port,
410	      cookieKey: parseCookieKeyString(config.http.cookieKey),
411	      allowedOrigins: config.http.allowedOrigins,
412	    }),
413	  );

content.content:
75	# `local` (the historical default): the host's `@ax/workspace-git` writes
76	# directly to the PVC mounted inside the host pod at workspace.mountPath.
77	# Single-replica only — two hosts would race for the same bare repo.
78	# `http`: the host's `@ax/workspace-git-http` forwards every workspace op
79	# over HTTP to the dedicated git-server pod, which is the unique writer.
80	# Multi-replica capable. When `http`, set gitServer.enabled=true (and the
81	# local PVC fields below stop mattering — they're not mounted).
82	workspace:
83	  backend: local         # local | http
84	  storage: 10Gi
85	  storageClassName: ""   # empty → cluster default
86	  accessMode: ReadWriteOnce
87	  mountPath: /var/lib/ax-next/workspaces
88	
89	# ─── Git-server pod (workspace.backend: http only) ────────────────
90	# Dedicated pod that owns the bare repo on a PVC. Every host replica talks
91	# to it for workspace ops. ALWAYS replicas: 1 — adding more would re-
92	# introduce the multi-writer race we're solving here. Not a knob.
93	gitServer:
94	  enabled: false         # set true when workspace.backend == "http"
95	  experimental:
96	    # Toggle for the new sharded git-server tier (Phase 1 of the workspace
97	    # redesign — see docs/plans/2026-05-01-workspace-redesign-design.md).
98	    # When true AND gitServer.enabled=true, the chart renders a StatefulSet
99	    # + headless Service + per-shard PVCs in addition to the legacy
100	    # Deployment + ClusterIP. Production canary path; default OFF.
101	    gitProtocol: false
102	  # Number of storage-tier shards. Each shard is its own StatefulSet ordinal
103	  # with its own PVC. Pick at install time; changing it later requires
104	  # manual workspace migration (drain → rsync bare repos → flip traffic).
105	  # Only consulted when experimental.gitProtocol=true.
106	  shards: 1
107	  port: 7780
108	  service:
109	    port: 7780
110	  storage: 10Gi
111	  storageClassName: ""   # empty → cluster default
112	  accessMode: ReadWriteOnce
113	  mountPath: /var/lib/ax-next/repo
114	  # Auth token: if empty, the chart generates a 48-char random token at
115	  # install time via the lookup-or-generate pattern. Operators can override
116	  # for deterministic deploys (e.g., GitOps).
117	  auth:
118	    token: ""
119	  resources:
120	    requests:
121	      cpu: 100m
122	      memory: 128Mi
123	    limits:
124	      cpu: 500m
125	      memory: 512Mi
126	  # Same image as host + runners — bundled-binary pattern.

file.content:
# `local` (the historical default): the host's `@ax/workspace-git` writes
# directly to the PVC mounted inside the host pod at workspace.mountPath.
# Single-replica only — two hosts would race for the same bare repo.
# `http`: the host's `@ax/workspace-git-http` forwards every workspace op
# over HTTP to the dedicated git-server pod, which is the unique writer.
# Multi-replica capable. When `http`, set gitServer.enabled=true (and the
# local PVC fields below stop mattering — they're not mounted).
workspace:
  backend: local         # local | http
  storage: 10Gi
  storageClassName: ""   # empty → cluster default
  accessMode: ReadWriteOnce
  mountPath: /var/lib/ax-next/workspaces

# ─── Git-server pod (workspace.backend: http only) ────────────────
# Dedicated pod that owns the bare repo on a PVC. Every host replica talks
# to it for workspace ops. ALWAYS replicas: 1 — adding more would re-
# introduce the multi-writer race we're solving here. Not a knob.
gitServer:
  enabled: false         # set true when workspace.backend == "http"
  experimental:
    # Toggle for the new sharded git-server tier (Phase 1 of the workspace
    # redesign — see docs/plans/2026-05-01-workspace-redesign-design.md).
    # When true AND gitServer.enabled=true, the chart renders a StatefulSet
    # + headless Service + per-shard PVCs in addition to the legacy
    # Deployment + ClusterIP. Production canary path; default OFF.
    gitProtocol: false
  # Number of storage-tier shards. Each shard is its own StatefulSet ordinal
  # with its own PVC. Pick at install time; changing it later requires
  # manual workspace migration (drain → rsync bare repos → flip traffic).
  # Only consulted when experimental.gitProtocol=true.
  shards: 1
  port: 7780
  service:
    port: 7780
  storage: 10Gi
  storageClassName: ""   # empty → cluster default
  accessMode: ReadWriteOnce
  mountPath: /var/lib/ax-next/repo
  # Auth token: if empty, the chart generates a 48-char random token at
  # install time via the lookup-or-generate pattern. Operators can override
  # for deterministic deploys (e.g., GitOps).
  auth:
    token: ""
  resources:
    requests:
      cpu: 100m
      memory: 128Mi
    limits:
      cpu: 500m
      memory: 512Mi
  # Same image as host + runners — bundled-binary pattern.
  command: ["node", "/opt/ax-next/git-server/index.js"]
  terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 60   # bumped from 30 — gives in-flight pushes more time to drain
  readinessProbe:
    httpGet:
      path: /healthz
      port: git
    initialDelaySeconds: 5
    periodSeconds: 10
  livenessProbe:
    httpGet:
      path: /healthz
      port: git
    initialDelaySeconds: 10
    periodSeconds: 30

# ─── Runner pod runtime ──────────────────────────────────────────
# `runtimeClassName` defaults to gvisor — the sandbox-k8s plugin's
# threat model assumes it. Override to "" for clusters without gVisor
# (you'll get a loud warning at host pod boot from the plugin).
sandbox:
  runtimeClassName: gvisor

# ─── PostgreSQL ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Two modes:
#   - external: false (default for kind-dev) → use the embedded subchart
#     (Bitnami postgresql). Subchart values flow through `postgresql:`.
#   - external: true → operator provides DATABASE_URL via an existing
#     Secret. Set `postgres.external.existingSecret` to its name.
#
# Single source of truth for embedded auth: `postgresql.auth.*` below.
# The Bitnami subchart owns those keys, so we don't duplicate them
# under `postgres.embedded.auth.*`. The host pod's DSN-building template
# and the init job both read from `postgresql.auth.*` so subchart and

content.text:
Plan saved to `docs/plans/2026-05-01-workspace-redesign-phase-2-plan.md`.

**Summary of what's in it:**

- **Goal:** Close Phase 1's half-wired window by promoting the test-only adapter into a registered production plugin (`createWorkspaceGitServerPlugin`), wiring it into `@ax/preset-k8s` + the Helm chart, all gated by the existing `gitServer.experimental.gitProtocol` toggle.
- **Six open questions for sign-off** before coding starts: plugin location, workspaceId derivation, mirror cache lifetime, retry policy, canary surface, acceptance test reach.
- **Key architectural decisions baked in:** SHA-256-derived `ws-<hex16>` workspaceIds from `(userId, agentId)`, per-workspace mirror cache with LRU eviction, retry policy mirroring `workspace-git-http`, third arm on `K8sWorkspaceConfig` (`backend: 'git-protocol'`), mutual-exclusion guardrails in the chart so misconfigured combos fail `helm template` loudly.
- **25 bite-sized TDD tasks** covering: workspaceId + shard router + retry helper + mirror cache + GitEngine extraction + production plugin factory + boundary-leak test + multi-replica concurrency + preset wiring + acceptance test + chart env-var branch + chart render tests + chart guardrails + SECURITY.md + runbook + PR body.
- **Boundary review, security review, migration/rollback, and the half-wired-window CLOSED template** all written out for the PR description.

**Two execution options:**

1. **Subagent-Driven (this session)** — I dispatch a fresh subagent per task, review between tasks, fast iteration.
2. **Parallel Session (separate)** — Open a new session with `superpowers:executing-plans`, batch execution with checkpoints.

Which approach? (And want me to first answer the six sign-off questions you'll have when you read the plan, or wait until you've read it?)

content.text: Going with Subagent-Driven execution. Taking your "keep going until done" as acceptance of all six recommended answers (same package; SHA-256 derived `ws-<hex16>` IDs; LRU mirror cache; mirrored retry policy; global toggle only; parallel acceptance test).

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/vpulim/.claude/plugins/cache/claude-plugins-official/superpowers/4.1.1/skills/subagent-driven-development

# Subagent-Driven Development

Execute plan by dispatching fresh subagent per task, with two-stage review after each: spec compliance review first, then code quality review.

**Core principle:** Fresh subagent per task + two-stage review (spec then quality) = high quality, fast iteration

## When to Use

```dot
digraph when_to_use {
    "Have implementation plan?" [shape=diamond];
    "Tasks mostly independent?" [shape=diamond];
    "Stay in this session?" [shape=diamond];
    "subagent-driven-development" [shape=box];
    "executing-plans" [shape=box];
    "Manual execution or brainstorm first" [shape=box];

    "Have implementation plan?" -> "Tasks mostly independent?" [label="yes"];
    "Have implementation plan?" -> "Manual execution or brainstorm first" [label="no"];
    "Tasks mostly independent?" -> "Stay in this session?" [label="yes"];
    "Tasks mostly independent?" -> "Manual execution or brainstorm first" [label="no - tightly coupled"];
    "Stay in this session?" -> "subagent-driven-development" [label="yes"];
    "Stay in this session?" -> "executing-plans" [label="no - parallel session"];
}
```

**vs. Executing Plans (parallel session):**
- Same session (no context switch)
- Fresh subagent per task (no context pollution)
- Two-stage review after each task: spec compliance first, then code quality
- Faster iteration (no human-in-loop between tasks)

## The Process

```dot
digraph process {
    rankdir=TB;

    subgraph cluster_per_task {
        label="Per Task";
        "Dispatch implementer subagent (./implementer-prompt.md)" [shape=box];
        "Implementer subagent asks questions?" [shape=diamond];
        "Answer questions, provide context" [shape=box];
        "Implementer subagent implements, tests, commits, self-reviews" [shape=box];
        "Dispatch spec reviewer subagent (./spec-reviewer-prompt.md)" [shape=box];
        "Spec reviewer subagent confirms code matches spec?" [shape=diamond];
        "Implementer subagent fixes spec gaps" [shape=box];
        "Dispatch code quality reviewer subagent (./code-quality-reviewer-prompt.md)" [shape=box];
        "Code quality reviewer subagent approves?" [shape=diamond];
        "Implementer subagent fixes quality issues" [shape=box];
        "Mark task complete in TodoWrite" [shape=box];
    }

    "Read plan, extract all tasks with full text, note context, create TodoWrite" [shape=box];
    "More tasks remain?" [shape=diamond];
    "Dispatch final code reviewer subagent for entire implementation" [shape=box];
    "Use superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch" [shape=box style=filled fillcolor=lightgreen];

    "Read plan, extract all tasks with full text, note context, create TodoWrite" -> "Dispatch implementer subagent (./implementer-prompt.md)";
    "Dispatch implementer subagent (./implementer-prompt.md)" -> "Implementer subagent asks questions?";
    "Implementer subagent asks questions?" -> "Answer questions, provide context" [label="yes"];
    "Answer questions, provide context" -> "Dispatch implementer subagent (./implementer-prompt.md)";
    "Implementer subagent asks questions?" -> "Implementer subagent implements, tests, commits, self-reviews" [label="no"];
    "Implementer subagent implements, tests, commits, self-reviews" -> "Dispatch spec reviewer subagent (./spec-reviewer-prompt.md)";
    "Dispatch spec reviewer subagent (./spec-reviewer-prompt.md)" -> "Spec reviewer subagent confirms code matches spec?";
    "Spec reviewer subagent confirms code matches spec?" -> "Implementer subagent fixes spec gaps" [label="no"];
    "Implementer subagent fixes spec gaps" -> "Dispatch spec reviewer subagent (./spec-reviewer-prompt.md)" [label="re-review"];
    "Spec reviewer subagent confirms code matches spec?" -> "Dispatch code quality reviewer subagent (./code-quality-reviewer-prompt.md)" [label="yes"];
    "Dispatch code quality reviewer subagent (./code-quality-reviewer-prompt.md)" -> "Code quality reviewer subagent approves?";
    "Code quality reviewer subagent approves?" -> "Implementer subagent fixes quality issues" [label="no"];
    "Implementer subagent fixes quality issues" -> "Dispatch code quality reviewer subagent (./code-quality-reviewer-prompt.md)" [label="re-review"];
    "Code quality reviewer subagent approves?" -> "Mark task complete in TodoWrite" [label="yes"];
    "Mark task complete in TodoWrite" -> "More tasks remain?";
    "More tasks remain?" -> "Dispatch implementer subagent (./implementer-prompt.md)" [label="yes"];
    "More tasks remain?" -> "Dispatch final code reviewer subagent for entire implementation" [label="no"];
    "Dispatch final code reviewer subagent for entire implementation" -> "Use superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch";
}
```

## Prompt Templates

- `./implementer-prompt.md` - Dispatch implementer subagent
- `./spec-reviewer-prompt.md` - Dispatch spec compliance reviewer subagent
- `./code-quality-reviewer-prompt.md` - Dispatch code quality reviewer subagent

## Example Workflow

```
You: I'm using Subagent-Driven Development to execute this plan.

[Read plan file once: docs/plans/feature-plan.md]
[Extract all 5 tasks with full text and context]
[Create TodoWrite with all tasks]

Task 1: Hook installation script

[Get Task 1 text and context (already extracted)]
[Dispatch implementation subagent with full task text + context]

Implementer: "Before I begin - should the hook be installed at user or system level?"

You: "User level (~/.config/superpowers/hooks/)"

Implementer: "Got it. Implementing now..."
[Later] Implementer:
  - Implemented install-hook command
  - Added tests, 5/5 passing
  - Self-review: Found I missed --force flag, added it
  - Committed

[Dispatch spec compliance reviewer]
Spec reviewer: ✅ Spec compliant - all requirements met, nothing extra

[Get git SHAs, dispatch code quality reviewer]
Code reviewer: Strengths: Good test coverage, clean. Issues: None. Approved.

[Mark Task 1 complete]

Task 2: Recovery modes

[Get Task 2 text and context (already extracted)]
[Dispatch implementation subagent with full task text + context]

Implementer: [No questions, proceeds]
Implementer:
  - Added verify/repair modes
  - 8/8 tests passing
  - Self-review: All good
  - Committed

[Dispatch spec compliance reviewer]
Spec reviewer: ❌ Issues:
  - Missing: Progress reporting (spec says "report every 100 items")
  - Extra: Added --json flag (not requested)

[Implementer fixes issues]
Implementer: Removed --json flag, added progress reporting

[Spec reviewer reviews again]
Spec reviewer: ✅ Spec compliant now

[Dispatch code quality reviewer]
Code reviewer: Strengths: Solid. Issues (Important): Magic number (100)

[Implementer fixes]
Implementer: Extracted PROGRESS_INTERVAL constant

[Code reviewer reviews again]
Code reviewer: ✅ Approved

[Mark Task 2 complete]

...

[After all tasks]
[Dispatch final code-reviewer]
Final reviewer: All requirements met, ready to merge

Done!
```

## Advantages

**vs. Manual execution:**
- Subagents follow TDD naturally
- Fresh context per task (no confusion)
- Parallel-safe (subagents don't interfere)
- Subagent can ask questions (before AND during work)

**vs. Executing Plans:**
- Same session (no handoff)
- Continuous progress (no waiting)
- Review checkpoints automatic

**Efficiency gains:**
- No file reading overhead (controller provides full text)
- Controller curates exactly what context is needed
- Subagent gets complete information upfront
- Questions surfaced before work begins (not after)

**Quality gates:**
- Self-review catches issues before handoff
- Two-stage review: spec compliance, then code quality
- Review loops ensure fixes actually work
- Spec compliance prevents over/under-building
- Code quality ensures implementation is well-built

**Cost:**
- More subagent invocations (implementer + 2 reviewers per task)
- Controller does more prep work (extracting all tasks upfront)
- Review loops add iterations
- But catches issues early (cheaper than debugging later)

## Red Flags

**Never:**
- Start implementation on main/master branch without explicit user consent
- Skip reviews (spec compliance OR code quality)
- Proceed with unfixed issues
- Dispatch multiple implementation subagents in parallel (conflicts)
- Make subagent read plan file (provide full text instead)
- Skip scene-setting context (subagent needs to understand where task fits)
- Ignore subagent questions (answer before letting them proceed)
- Accept "close enough" on spec compliance (spec reviewer found issues = not done)
- Skip review loops (reviewer found issues = implementer fixes = review again)
- Let implementer self-review replace actual review (both are needed)
- **Start code quality review before spec compliance is ✅** (wrong order)
- Move to next task while either review has open issues

**If subagent asks questions:**
- Answer clearly and completely
- Provide additional context if needed
- Don't rush them into implementation

**If reviewer finds issues:**
- Implementer (same subagent) fixes them
- Reviewer reviews again
- Repeat until approved
- Don't skip the re-review

**If subagent fails task:**
- Dispatch fix subagent with specific instructions
- Don't try to fix manually (context pollution)

## Integration

**Required workflow skills:**
- **superpowers:using-git-worktrees** - REQUIRED: Set up isolated workspace before starting
- **superpowers:writing-plans** - Creates the plan this skill executes
- **superpowers:requesting-code-review** - Code review template for reviewer subagents
- **superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch** - Complete development after all tasks

**Subagents should use:**
- **superpowers:test-driven-development** - Subagents follow TDD for each task

**Alternative workflow:**
- **superpowers:executing-plans** - Use for parallel session instead of same-session execution
I'm on main — need an isolated worktree before starting. Let me load the worktree skill and the prompt templates.

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/vpulim/.claude/plugins/cache/claude-plugins-official/superpowers/4.1.1/skills/using-git-worktrees

# Using Git Worktrees

## Overview

Git worktrees create isolated workspaces sharing the same repository, allowing work on multiple branches simultaneously without switching.

**Core principle:** Systematic directory selection + safety verification = reliable isolation.

**Announce at start:** "I'm using the using-git-worktrees skill to set up an isolated workspace."

## Directory Selection Process

Follow this priority order:

### 1. Check Existing Directories

```bash
# Check in priority order
ls -d .worktrees 2>/dev/null     # Preferred (hidden)
ls -d worktrees 2>/dev/null      # Alternative
```

**If found:** Use that directory. If both exist, `.worktrees` wins.

### 2. Check CLAUDE.md

```bash
grep -i "worktree.*director" CLAUDE.md 2>/dev/null
```

**If preference specified:** Use it without asking.

### 3. Ask User

If no directory exists and no CLAUDE.md preference:

```
No worktree directory found. Where should I create worktrees?

1. .worktrees/ (project-local, hidden)
2. ~/.config/superpowers/worktrees/<project-name>/ (global location)

Which would you prefer?
```

## Safety Verification

### For Project-Local Directories (.worktrees or worktrees)

**MUST verify directory is ignored before creating worktree:**

```bash
# Check if directory is ignored (respects local, global, and system gitignore)
git check-ignore -q .worktrees 2>/dev/null || git check-ignore -q worktrees 2>/dev/null
```

**If NOT ignored:**

Per Jesse's rule "Fix broken things immediately":
1. Add appropriate line to .gitignore
2. Commit the change
3. Proceed with worktree creation

**Why critical:** Prevents accidentally committing worktree contents to repository.

### For Global Directory (~/.config/superpowers/worktrees)

No .gitignore verification needed - outside project entirely.

## Creation Steps

### 1. Detect Project Name

```bash
project=$(basename "$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)")
```

### 2. Create Worktree

```bash
# Determine full path
case $LOCATION in
  .worktrees|worktrees)
    path="$LOCATION/$BRANCH_NAME"
    ;;
  ~/.config/superpowers/worktrees/*)
    path="~/.config/superpowers/worktrees/$project/$BRANCH_NAME"
    ;;
esac

# Create worktree with new branch
git worktree add "$path" -b "$BRANCH_NAME"
cd "$path"
```

### 3. Run Project Setup

Auto-detect and run appropriate setup:

```bash
# Node.js
if [ -f package.json ]; then npm install; fi

# Rust
if [ -f Cargo.toml ]; then cargo build; fi

# Python
if [ -f requirements.txt ]; then pip install -r requirements.txt; fi
if [ -f pyproject.toml ]; then poetry install; fi

# Go
if [ -f go.mod ]; then go mod download; fi
```

### 4. Verify Clean Baseline

Run tests to ensure worktree starts clean:

```bash
# Examples - use project-appropriate command
npm test
cargo test
pytest
go test ./...
```

**If tests fail:** Report failures, ask whether to proceed or investigate.

**If tests pass:** Report ready.

### 5. Report Location

```
Worktree ready at <full-path>
Tests passing (<N> tests, 0 failures)
Ready to implement <feature-name>
```

## Quick Reference

| Situation | Action |
|-----------|--------|
| `.worktrees/` exists | Use it (verify ignored) |
| `worktrees/` exists | Use it (verify ignored) |
| Both exist | Use `.worktrees/` |
| Neither exists | Check CLAUDE.md → Ask user |
| Directory not ignored | Add to .gitignore + commit |
| Tests fail during baseline | Report failures + ask |
| No package.json/Cargo.toml | Skip dependency install |

## Common Mistakes

### Skipping ignore verification

- **Problem:** Worktree contents get tracked, pollute git status
- **Fix:** Always use `git check-ignore` before creating project-local worktree

### Assuming directory location

- **Problem:** Creates inconsistency, violates project conventions
- **Fix:** Follow priority: existing > CLAUDE.md > ask

### Proceeding with failing tests

- **Problem:** Can't distinguish new bugs from pre-existing issues
- **Fix:** Report failures, get explicit permission to proceed

### Hardcoding setup commands

- **Problem:** Breaks on projects using different tools
- **Fix:** Auto-detect from project files (package.json, etc.)

## Example Workflow

```
You: I'm using the using-git-worktrees skill to set up an isolated workspace.

[Check .worktrees/ - exists]
[Verify ignored - git check-ignore confirms .worktrees/ is ignored]
[Create worktree: git worktree add .worktrees/auth -b feature/auth]
[Run npm install]
[Run npm test - 47 passing]

Worktree ready at /Users/jesse/myproject/.worktrees/auth
Tests passing (47 tests, 0 failures)
Ready to implement auth feature
```

## Red Flags

**Never:**
- Create worktree without verifying it's ignored (project-local)
- Skip baseline test verification
- Proceed with failing tests without asking
- Assume directory location when ambiguous
- Skip CLAUDE.md check

**Always:**
- Follow directory priority: existing > CLAUDE.md > ask
- Verify directory is ignored for project-local
- Auto-detect and run project setup
- Verify clean test baseline

## Integration

**Called by:**
- **brainstorming** (Phase 4) - REQUIRED when design is approved and implementation follows
- **subagent-driven-development** - REQUIRED before executing any tasks
- **executing-plans** - REQUIRED before executing any tasks
- Any skill needing isolated workspace

**Pairs with:**
- **finishing-a-development-branch** - REQUIRED for cleanup after work complete

# Implementer Subagent Prompt Template

Use this template when dispatching an implementer subagent.

```
Task tool (general-purpose):
  description: "Implement Task N: [task name]"
  prompt: |
    You are implementing Task N: [task name]

    ## Task Description

    [FULL TEXT of task from plan - paste it here, don't make subagent read file]

    ## Context

    [Scene-setting: where this fits, dependencies, architectural context]

    ## Before You Begin

    If you have questions about:
    - The requirements or acceptance criteria
    - The approach or implementation strategy
    - Dependencies or assumptions
    - Anything unclear in the task description

    **Ask them now.** Raise any concerns before starting work.

    ## Your Job

    Once you're clear on requirements:
    1. Implement exactly what the task specifies
    2. Write tests (following TDD if task says to)
    3. Verify implementation works
    4. Commit your work
    5. Self-review (see below)
    6. Report back

    Work from: [directory]

    **While you work:** If you encounter something unexpected or unclear, **ask questions**.
    It's always OK to pause and clarify. Don't guess or make assumptions.

    ## Before Reporting Back: Self-Review

    Review your work with fresh eyes. Ask yourself:

    **Completeness:**
    - Did I fully implement everything in the spec?
    - Did I miss any requirements?
    - Are there edge cases I didn't handle?

    **Quality:**
    - Is this my best work?
    - Are names clear and accurate (match what things do, not how they work)?
    - Is the code clean and maintainable?

    **Discipline:**
    - Did I avoid overbuilding (YAGNI)?
    - Did I only build what was requested?
    - Did I follow existing patterns in the codebase?

    **Testing:**
    - Do tests actually verify behavior (not just mock behavior)?
    - Did I follow TDD if required?
    - Are tests comprehensive?

    If you find issues during self-review, fix them now before reporting.

    ## Report Format

    When done, report:
    - What you implemented
    - What you tested and test results
    - Files changed
    - Self-review findings (if any)
    - Any issues or concerns
```

# Spec Compliance Reviewer Prompt Template

Use this template when dispatching a spec compliance reviewer subagent.

**Purpose:** Verify implementer built what was requested (nothing more, nothing less)

```
Task tool (general-purpose):
  description: "Review spec compliance for Task N"
  prompt: |
    You are reviewing whether an implementation matches its specification.

    ## What Was Requested

    [FULL TEXT of task requirements]

    ## What Implementer Claims They Built

    [From implementer's report]

    ## CRITICAL: Do Not Trust the Report

    The implementer finished suspiciously quickly. Their report may be incomplete,
    inaccurate, or optimistic. You MUST verify everything independently.

    **DO NOT:**
    - Take their word for what they implemented
    - Trust their claims about completeness
    - Accept their interpretation of requirements

    **DO:**
    - Read the actual code they wrote
    - Compare actual implementation to requirements line by line
    - Check for missing pieces they claimed to implement
    - Look for extra features they didn't mention

    ## Your Job

    Read the implementation code and verify:

    **Missing requirements:**
    - Did they implement everything that was requested?
    - Are there requirements they skipped or missed?
    - Did they claim something works but didn't actually implement it?

    **Extra/unneeded work:**
    - Did they build things that weren't requested?
    - Did they over-engineer or add unnecessary features?
    - Did they add "nice to haves" that weren't in spec?

    **Misunderstandings:**
    - Did they interpret requirements differently than intended?
    - Did they solve the wrong problem?
    - Did they implement the right feature but wrong way?

    **Verify by reading code, not by trusting report.**

    Report:
    - ✅ Spec compliant (if everything matches after code inspection)
    - ❌ Issues found: [list specifically what's missing or extra, with file:line references]
```

input.prompt:
You are implementing Tasks 1 and 2 of the Phase 2 workspace redesign plan: workspaceId derivation (failing test, then impl).

## Working directory

`/Users/vpulim/dev/ai/ax-next/.worktrees/feat-phase-2-workspace-host-plugin`

This is a git worktree on branch `feat/phase-2-workspace-host-plugin`. Run all commands and edits from this path. **Never** modify the main repo at `/Users/vpulim/dev/ai/ax-next/` directly.

## Task 1: workspaceId derivation (failing test)

**File to create:** `packages/workspace-git-server/src/client/__tests__/workspace-id.test.ts`

Tests required:
- **Determinism:** `workspaceIdFor({ userId: 'u', agentId: 'a' })` returns the same value across 1000 calls.
- **Regex match:** 100 hand-chosen `(userId, agentId)` pairs (incl. unicode, very long, empty-ish) all produce a value matching `WORKSPACE_ID_REGEX` exported from `../../shared/workspace-id.js`.
- **Distinct:** `(u1, a)` ≠ `(u2, a)`; `(u, a1)` ≠ `(u, a2)` (no agentId-only or userId-only collisions).
- **Pinned outputs:** 5 hand-chosen `(userId, agentId)` pairs map to 5 hardcoded expected workspaceIds. (Captures derivation drift across SHA-256 changes.)

For the pinned outputs: compute the expected SHA-256 manually using `node -e` or similar BEFORE the impl exists. Format: `ws-` + first 16 hex chars of `sha256(userId + '/' + agentId)`. Example pairs:
- `{userId: 'alice', agentId: 'agent-1'}` 
- `{userId: 'bob', agentId: 'agent-1'}`
- `{userId: 'alice', agentId: 'agent-2'}`
- `{userId: '', agentId: ''}`
- `{userId: 'user-with-/-slash', agentId: 'agent-x'}` — confirms the userId/agentId are concatenated with `/` separator (note this case has the same hash as `{userId: 'user-with-', agentId: 'slash/agent-x'}`; this is a known and acceptable property — document it in a comment in the test file)

Run: `pnpm --filter @ax/workspace-git-server test` → must FAIL because `../workspace-id.js` doesn't exist yet.

**Commit:** `test(workspace-git-server): workspaceId derivation spec`

## Task 2: workspaceId derivation (impl)

**File to create:** `packages/workspace-git-server/src/client/workspace-id.ts`

```ts
import { createHash } from 'node:crypto';

export function workspaceIdFor(ctx: { userId: string; agentId: string }): string {
  const h = createHash('sha256')
    .update(ctx.userId)
    .update('/')
    .update(ctx.agentId)
    .digest('hex');
  return `ws-${h.slice(0, 16)}`;
}
```

Add a brief comment block at the top explaining:
- This derives a stable workspaceId from `(userId, agentId)`.
- The output always matches `WORKSPACE_ID_REGEX` (`^[a-z0-9][a-z0-9_-]{0,62}$`): starts with `w`, contains only `[a-z0-9-]`, length 19.
- Stability is load-bearing: changing the derivation post-deploy would orphan every existing workspace's bare repo on the storage tier shards. Pinned-output tests catch unintentional drift.
- Concatenation uses `/` as separator. The known collision `{userId:'a', agentId:'b/c'}` ≡ `{userId:'a/b', agentId:'c'}` is acceptable because both `userId` and `agentId` come from authenticated sessions where a malicious user can't choose a userId to collide with someone else's `(userId, agentId)`.

Run: `pnpm --filter @ax/workspace-git-server test` → tests pass. Confirm test count went up.

**Commit:** `feat(workspace-git-server): workspaceId derivation`

## Architectural context

This is part of Phase 2 of the workspace redesign (`docs/plans/2026-05-01-workspace-redesign-phase-2-plan.md`). The new function will be used by Phase 2's production host plugin to derive a workspaceId per `workspace:apply` call from `AgentContext.userId` and `AgentContext.agentId`. The shape `^[a-z0-9][a-z0-9_-]{0,62}$` is enforced by the storage tier (`packages/workspace-git-server/src/shared/workspace-id.ts:WORKSPACE_ID_REGEX`); our derivation must always satisfy it.

Key codebase conventions to follow:
- Vitest for tests (see existing `*.test.ts` files in the package for shape).
- TypeScript strict mode; no `any` without `eslint-disable`.
- Commit messages match the package's existing style (see `git log --oneline | head -20`).
- Files use `.js` import extensions even for `.ts` (Node ESM).
- Tests live under `__tests__/` co-located with source.

Before starting, read these for shape reference:
- `packages/workspace-git-server/src/shared/workspace-id.ts` (the regex you'll import).
- `packages/workspace-git-server/src/shared/__tests__/workspace-id.test.ts` (test shape conventions).
- `packages/workspace-git-server/package.json` (deps available — only `@ax/core` and node stdlib should be needed; `crypto` is node stdlib).

## Before You Begin

If you have questions about:
- The exact pinned hash values (you should compute these yourself; don't ask me)
- The regex import path
- Test framework conventions
- The two-commit structure (one for the failing test, one for the impl)

**Ask them now.** Otherwise proceed.

## Your Job

1. Implement Task 1 (failing test) and verify it fails for the right reason (module missing).
2. Commit Task 1.
3. Implement Task 2 (impl).
4. Verify Task 1's test passes and the broader package test suite still passes (`pnpm --filter @ax/workspace-git-server test` → all green, count went UP).
5. Commit Task 2.
6. Self-review per the criteria below.
7. Report back.

## Before reporting: Self-Review

- **Completeness:** All four test categories present (determinism, regex, distinct, 5 pinned)? Comment in workspace-id.ts explains stability + collision property?
- **Quality:** Names clear (`workspaceIdFor` matches existing `shardForWorkspace` shape)? No throwaway code or commented-out lines?
- **Discipline:** No extra exports beyond `workspaceIdFor`. No re-exporting `WORKSPACE_ID_REGEX`. No unrelated changes.
- **Testing:** Tests verify behavior (the actual hash values are pinned, not just "any valid output"). All 100 regex-match cases use real strings, not `expect.toMatch` against the regex source.

## Report Format

Report:
- What you implemented (1-2 sentences).
- `pnpm --filter @ax/workspace-git-server test` output: before-count → after-count.
- Files changed (paths only).
- Two commit SHAs (run `git log --oneline -3` and paste).
- Any self-review findings or fixes.
- Any concerns.

content.text:
Both commits in place. Self-review pass:

**Completeness:**
- All four test categories present: determinism (1000 calls), regex (100 cases), distinct (userId-only and agentId-only collision checks), 5 pinned outputs.
- Comment in `workspace-id.ts` explains: stability load-bearing rationale, regex shape, separator, known collision property with the realistic example.

**Quality:**
- `workspaceIdFor` matches the existing `shardForWorkspace` shape (compute-from-id helper).
- No throwaway code or commented-out lines.

**Discipline:**
- Only export from impl: `workspaceIdFor`.

- **Distinct:** `(u1, a)` ≠ `(u2, a)`; `(u, a1)` ≠ `(u, a2)` (no agentId-only or userId-only collisions).
- **Pinned outputs:** 5 hand-chosen `(userId, agentId)` pairs map to 5 hardcoded expected workspaceIds. (Captures derivation drift across SHA-256 changes.)

For the pinned outputs: compute the expected SHA-256 manually using `node -e` or similar BEFORE the impl exists. Format: `ws-` + first 16 hex chars of `sha256(userId + '/' + agentId)`. Example pairs:
- `{userId: 'alice', agentId: 'agent-1'}` 
- `{userId: 'bob', agentId: 'agent-1'}`
- `{userId: 'alice', agentId: 'agent-2'}`
- `{userId: '', agentId: ''}`
- `{userId: 'user-with-/-slash', agentId: 'agent-x'}` — confirms the userId/agentId are concatenated with `/` separator (note this case has the same hash as `{userId: 'user-with-', agentId: 'slash/agent-x'}`; this is a known and acceptable property — document it in a comment in the test file)

Run: `pnpm --filter @ax/workspace-git-server test` → must FAIL because `../workspace-id.js` doesn't exist yet.

**Commit:** `test(workspace-git-server): workspaceId derivation spec`

## Task 2: workspaceId derivation (impl)

**File to create:** `packages/workspace-git-server/src/client/workspace-id.ts`

```ts
import { createHash } from 'node:crypto';

export function workspaceIdFor(ctx: { userId: string; agentId: string }): string {
  const h = createHash('sha256')
    .update(ctx.userId)
    .update('/')
    .update(ctx.agentId)
    .digest('hex');
  return `ws-${h.slice(0, 16)}`;
}
```

Add a brief comment block at the top explaining:
- This derives a stable workspaceId from `(userId, agentId)`.
- The output always matches `WORKSPACE_ID_REGEX` (`^[a-z0-9][a-z0-9_-]{0,62}$`): starts with `w`, contains only `[a-z0-9-]`, length 19.
- Stability is load-bearing: changing the derivation post-deploy would orphan every existing workspace's bare repo on the storage tier shards. Pinned-output tests catch unintentional drift.
- Concatenation uses `/` as separator. The known collision `{userId:'a', agentId:'b/c'}` ≡ `{userId:'a/b', agentId:'c'}` is acceptable because both `userId` and `agentId` come from authenticated sessions where a malicious user can't choose a userId to collide with someone else's `(userId, agentId)`.

Run: `pnpm --filter @ax/workspace-git-server test` → tests pass. Confirm test count went up.

**Commit:** `feat(workspace-git-server): workspaceId derivation`

Output length 19, only `[a-z0-9-]` chars, starts with `w` — satisfies `^[a-z0-9][a-z0-9_-]{0,62}$`. Imports only `createHash` from `node:crypto`. Exports only `workspaceIdFor`. No CLI/config/README/extras.

4. Test file (`workspace-id.test.ts`):
   - Imports `workspaceIdFor` from `../workspace-id.js` (line 2) and `WORKSPACE_ID_REGEX` from `../../shared/workspace-id.js` (line 3) — both with `.js` extension as required, and the regex is imported directly in the test rather than re-exported from the impl module.
   - **Determinism** (line 111-118): runs 1000 iterations.
   - **Regex match** (line 120-128): `REGEX_CASES.length` is asserted to be exactly 100 (line 122), and I counted 100 entries (lines 9-108). Cases include unicode, very long (10k chars), empty-ish, control chars, NUL, lone surrogates, path traversal, SQL injection, etc. All assert `WORKSPACE_ID_REGEX.test(id)` against real strings — no `expect.toMatch` against regex source.
   - **Distinct** (line 130-142): both `(u1,a)≠(u2,a)` and `(u,a1)≠(u,a2)` covered.
   - **Pinned outputs** (line 157-165): exactly 5 cases via `it.each`. I independently computed all five with `node`+`crypto` and they match byte-for-byte.

5. Test count: 404 passed across 15 files (matches the +9 claim, 395→404).

6. Binary git classification: real, not a mistake. The test file contains NUL bytes (line 27: `'user\x00with\x00nul'`) and lone surrogate halves (line 89: `\ud83d` and `\ude00`), which makes git treat it as binary and `file(1)` classify it as `data`. This is a side effect of genuinely testing adversarial inputs — fixing it would require dropping those cases. Acceptable as-is; the file still parses, runs, and TypeScript reads it fine.

7. Comment in impl (lines 16-21) correctly documents the collision shape `(a, b/c) ≡ (a/b, c)` (both produce sha256 of `a/b/c`), with justification rooted in the authentication trust boundary.

Nothing missing, nothing extra, no misunderstandings.

Verification complete. Both commits exist with correct messages, only the two intended files were touched, the impl exports only `workspaceIdFor`, the test imports `WORKSPACE_ID_REGEX` directly from `../../shared/workspace-id.js` (not re-exported through the impl), and tests run green at 404 across 15 files.

## Report

**Status: Spec compliant.**

**Verification results:**

1. Commits present and correctly named:
   - `9addbae test(workspace-git-server): workspaceId derivation spec`
   - `048294c feat(workspace-git-server): workspaceId derivation`

2. Diff scope: only the two intended files added; no other files touched.
   - `packages/workspace-git-server/src/client/__tests__/workspace-id.test.ts` (new, Bin 6356 bytes)
   - `packages/workspace-git-server/src/client/workspace-id.ts` (+32 lines)

3. Impl (`workspace-id.ts:25-32`): uses `createHash('sha256').update(userId).update('/').update(agentId).digest('hex').slice(0,16)` exactly as specified, prefixed `ws-`. Output length 19, only `[a-z0-9-]` chars, starts with `w` — satisfies `^[a-z0-9][a-z0-9_-]{0,62}$`. Imports only `createHash` from `node:crypto`. Exports only `workspaceIdFor`. No CLI/config/README/extras.

4. Test file (`workspace-id.test.ts`):
   - Imports `workspaceIdFor` from `../workspace-id.js` (line 2) and `WORKSPACE_ID_REGEX` from `../../shared/workspace-id.js` (line 3) — both with `.js` extension as required, and the regex is imported directly in the test rather than re-exported from the impl module.
   - **Determinism** (line 111-118): runs 1000 iterations.
   - **Regex match** (line 120-128): `REGEX_CASES.length` is asserted to be exactly 100 (line 122), and I counted 100 entries (lines 9-108). Cases include unicode, very long (10k chars), empty-ish, control chars, NUL, lone surrogates, path traversal, SQL injection, etc. All assert `WORKSPACE_ID_REGEX.test(id)` against real strings — no `expect.toMatch` against regex source.
   - **Distinct** (line 130-142): both `(u1,a)≠(u2,a)` and `(u,a1)≠(u,a2)` covered.
   - **Pinned outputs** (line 157-165): exactly 5 cases via `it.each`. I independently computed all five with `node`+`crypto` and they match byte-for-byte.

5. Test count: 404 passed across 15 files (matches the +9 claim, 395→404).

6. Binary git classification: real, not a mistake. The test file contains NUL bytes (line 27: `'user\x00with\x00nul'`) and lone surrogate halves (line 89: `\ud83d` and `\ude00`), which makes git treat it as binary and `file(1)` classify it as `data`. This is a side effect of genuinely testing adversarial inputs — fixing it would require dropping those cases. Acceptable as-is; the file still parses, runs, and TypeScript reads it fine.

7. Comment in impl (lines 16-21) correctly documents the collision shape `(a, b/c) ≡ (a/b, c)` (both produce sha256 of `a/b/c`), with justification rooted in the authentication trust boundary.

Nothing missing, nothing extra, no misunderstandings.
Spec compliant. Now dispatching code quality reviewer.

## What was implemented

Tasks 1 and 2 of the Phase 2 workspace redesign plan (`docs/plans/2026-05-01-workspace-redesign-phase-2-plan.md`):

- Task 2 (commit `048294c`): impl of `workspaceIdFor({userId, agentId})` returning `ws-<first 16 hex of sha256(userId + '/' + agentId)>`.

## Requirements

Per the Phase 2 plan, this derivation:
- Always produces a string matching `WORKSPACE_ID_REGEX` (`^[a-z0-9][a-z0-9_-]{0,62}$`).
- Is deterministic — load-bearing because changing it post-deploy would orphan every existing workspace's bare repo on the storage tier shards.
- Concatenates with `/` as separator.
- The known collision `(a, b/c) ≡ (a/b, c)` is acceptable because both userId and agentId come from authenticated sessions where a malicious user can't choose a userId.

## Git range to review

**Base:** `9078b9f` (the merge of Phase 1)
**Head:** `048294c` (current HEAD)

```bash
git diff --stat 9078b9f..048294c
git diff 9078b9f..048294c
```

## Project conventions to enforce (from CLAUDE.md)

- **The five invariants:** I1 (no storage vocab in hook payloads), I2 (no cross-plugin imports), I3 (no half-wired plugins), I4 (one source of truth), I5 (capabilities minimized).
- TypeScript strict mode; `any` requires `eslint-disable` comment with reason.
- Files use `.js` import extensions (Node ESM).
- Don't over-comment: explain WHY, not WHAT. Comments only when removing them would confuse a future reader.
- No backwards-compat shims, no half-implemented features.
- Names match what things do, not how they work.

## Review checklist

- Clean separation of concerns?
- Proper error handling? (For this small utility, mostly: does it handle empty strings, very long strings, weird unicode without panicking?)
- Type safety? (No `any` without justification.)
- DRY principle followed?
- Edge cases handled?

**Architecture:**
- Sound design? (Deterministic SHA-256 derivation; immune to ordering attacks.)
- Performance? (SHA-256 of two strings — constant-time-ish, fine for hot path.)
- Security? (No secrets in this code; userId+agentId are not secret. The test's adversarial cases — NUL bytes, control chars — should not affect the impl's correctness.)

**Testing:**
- Tests verify behavior (pinned hash values), not just shape?
- Edge cases covered (unicode, long, empty)?
- Integration tests where needed? (N/A — pure function.)
- All tests passing? (Confirmed: 404 passing.)

**Requirements:**
- All plan requirements met?
- Implementation matches spec?
- No scope creep?

**Production readiness:**
- Comments explain stability load-bearing-ness so a future engineer doesn't "just refactor" the derivation?
- No obvious bugs?
- Imports use `.js` extension?

## Review: Phase 2 Tasks 1 + 2 (workspaceIdFor)

**Files reviewed:**
- `/Users/vpulim/dev/ai/ax-next/.worktrees/feat-phase-2-workspace-host-plugin/packages/workspace-git-server/src/client/workspace-id.ts`
- `/Users/vpulim/dev/ai/ax-next/.worktrees/feat-phase-2-workspace-host-plugin/packages/workspace-git-server/src/client/__tests__/workspace-id.test.ts`

### Strengths

- **Spec-first discipline executed cleanly.** Task 1 lands the spec (commit `9addbae`); Task 2 makes it pass (commit `048294c`). The 5 pinned hash values were independently verified by recomputing `sha256('userId/agentId')[0..16]` outside the codebase — every one matches.
- **Pinned-output suite is the right shape for a load-bearing derivation.** The test names + comments explicitly call out *why* drift would be catastrophic ("orphan every existing workspace's bare repo on the storage tier shards"), and `it.each` makes the contract auditable at a glance. This is exactly the kind of test that earns its keep.
- **Output is provably regex-safe by construction.** `ws-` + 16 lowercase hex characters → length 19, every char in `[a-z0-9-]`, leading char `w`. Cannot fail `WORKSPACE_ID_REGEX`. The 100-case fuzz suite (unicode, lone surrogates, 10k-char inputs, NUL bytes, traversal patterns, shell metas) confirms it empirically as well as by construction. I ran a few of the gnarliest cases (lone surrogate `\ud83d`, 10k chars, embedded NUL) directly against the impl — none threw, all matched the regex.
- **Streaming `.update()` calls avoid an intermediate concatenation.** Slightly nicer for very long inputs than `.update(userId + '/' + agentId)`, and produces an identical hash.
- **Doc comment is exactly the right kind of "WHY" comment** that CLAUDE.md asks for: explains the load-bearing nature, the known `(a, b/c) ≡ (a/b, c)` collision, and *why* that collision is acceptable (authenticated sessions, no attacker-chosen userIds). A future engineer who reads this won't "just refactor" the derivation, which is the goal.
- **Imports use `.js` extensions** (`'../workspace-id.js'`, `'../../shared/workspace-id.js'`). Conforms to project ESM convention.
- **No `any`, no cross-plugin imports** (only imports `node:crypto` and the package's own `shared/workspace-id.js`). Five-invariants clean.
- All 404 tests in the package pass.

### Issues

#### Critical (Must Fix)

None.

#### Important (Should Fix)

None.

#### Minor (Nice to Have)

**1. `client/` placement vs. `shared/` placement — confidence ~70, below report threshold but worth flagging for the next task.** The existing `WORKSPACE_ID_REGEX` and `validateWorkspaceId` live in `src/shared/workspace-id.ts` and are imported by both server-side code (`server/smart-http.ts`, `server/repos.ts`) and now the client-side test. The new `workspaceIdFor` lives in `src/client/workspace-id.ts`. Two files named `workspace-id.ts` in sibling directories is a minor speed-bump on grep. If future tasks need the server side to *also* call `workspaceIdFor` (e.g., a server-side admin tool, or a contract test that asserts a server-rendered URL matches `workspaceIdFor(...)`), the split becomes awkward. Not a blocker — Task 2's plan placement is in `client/` for a reason (clients derive IDs; the server only validates them) — but worth noting if a server-side caller appears later.

**2. Comment in `workspace-id.ts` line 17–18 has a small wording redundancy.** "pairs whose userId+'/'+agentId concatenation is identical hash to identical values" reads slightly garbled. The test file's version (line 153–156) is cleaner: "pairs whose concatenated form is identical hash to identical values." Same idea, equally awkward — both could be tightened to "pairs whose `userId + '/' + agentId` concatenation is identical produce identical hashes." Pure nit; do not block on this.

### Assessment

**Production ready — Approved.**

Tasks 1 and 2 are exactly what the plan asked for, executed test-first, with pinned outputs that lock the derivation against silent drift. The implementation is 7 lines of obvious code, the comment explains the load-bearing-ness, and the test suite is thorough enough that an accidental refactor would fail loudly. No critical or important issues. Ship.

You are implementing Tasks 3 and 4 of the Phase 2 workspace redesign plan: shard-router URL composition (failing test, then impl).

## Working directory

`/Users/vpulim/dev/ai/ax-next/.worktrees/feat-phase-2-workspace-host-plugin`

This is a git worktree on branch `feat/phase-2-workspace-host-plugin`. Run all commands from this path. **Never** modify the main repo at `/Users/vpulim/dev/ai/ax-next/` directly.

## Task 3: shard-router URL composition (failing test)

**File to create:** `packages/workspace-git-server/src/client/__tests__/shard-router.test.ts`

Tests required:
- `shardUrl({ serviceName: 'gs', namespace: 'ns', port: 7780, shardIndex: 0 })` returns `http://gs-0.gs.ns.svc.cluster.local:7780`.
- Same for shardIndex 1, 2, ... up to 31 (loop with `it.each` or similar). Each shardIndex N produces `http://<serviceName>-<N>.<serviceName>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local:<port>`.
- `shardUrlForWorkspace(workspaceId, opts)` composes `shardForWorkspace(workspaceId, opts.shards)` with `shardUrl({...opts, shardIndex})` — for a few hand-picked workspaceIds, the returned URL matches `shardUrl({serviceName, namespace, port, shardIndex: shardForWorkspace(wsId, shards)})`.
- Determinism: `shardUrlForWorkspace('ws-abc', {serviceName, namespace, port, shards: 4})` returns the same URL across 100 calls.
- Different `shards` count produces different shard distributions for the same workspaceId set (sanity that the function actually reads `opts.shards`).

Run: `pnpm --filter @ax/workspace-git-server test` → must FAIL because `../shard-router.js` doesn't exist yet.

**Commit:** `test(workspace-git-server): shard-router URL composition spec`

## Task 4: shard-router impl

**File to create:** `packages/workspace-git-server/src/client/shard-router.ts`

Implementation per the Phase 2 plan:

```ts
import { shardForWorkspace } from '../shared/shard.js';

export interface ShardLocator {
  serviceName: string;
  namespace: string;
  port: number;
  shards: number;
}

export function shardUrl(opts: {
  serviceName: string;
  namespace: string;
  port: number;
  shardIndex: number;
}): string {
  return `http://${opts.serviceName}-${opts.shardIndex}.${opts.serviceName}.${opts.namespace}.svc.cluster.local:${opts.port}`;
}

export function shardUrlForWorkspace(workspaceId: string, opts: ShardLocator): string {
  const shardIndex = shardForWorkspace(workspaceId, opts.shards);
  return shardUrl({ ...opts, shardIndex });
}
```

Add a brief comment block at the top explaining:
- These functions compose Kubernetes-headless-Service per-pod DNS names. The shape `<serviceName>-<N>.<serviceName>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local` is what a `StatefulSet` + headless `Service` produces by default (per-replica stable DNS).
- `shardUrlForWorkspace` is the production hot path: given a workspaceId, return the URL of the storage tier shard that owns it.
- Inputs are NOT validated here. Callers should validate `serviceName` / `namespace` / `port` at config-load time (in `@ax/preset-k8s`), not on every hook call. workspaceId is derived from `workspaceIdFor` upstream and is therefore regex-safe.

Run: `pnpm --filter @ax/workspace-git-server test` → tests pass. Confirm test count went up.

**Commit:** `feat(workspace-git-server): shard-router URL composition`

## Architectural context

This is part of Phase 2 of the workspace redesign (`docs/plans/2026-05-01-workspace-redesign-phase-2-plan.md`). The function `shardUrlForWorkspace` will be the production hot path for the new host plugin to look up "which storage tier shard owns this workspace." Phase 1 already shipped `shardForWorkspace(workspaceId, shards)` returning a numeric shard index (sha256 first 4 bytes mod N) at `packages/workspace-git-server/src/shared/shard.ts`.

The DNS shape comes from how Kubernetes renders a StatefulSet + headless Service: `<sts-name>-<ordinal>.<headless-service-name>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local`. In our chart, the StatefulSet name and the headless Service name are intentionally the same (e.g., `ax-next-git-server`), so callers pass one `serviceName` value.

Key codebase conventions:
- TypeScript strict mode; no `any`.
- Files use `.js` import extensions even for `.ts` (Node ESM).
- Tests use Vitest.
- Tests live under `__tests__/` co-located with source.

Before starting, read these for shape reference:
- `packages/workspace-git-server/src/shared/shard.ts` (the function you'll import).
- `packages/workspace-git-server/src/shared/__tests__/shard.test.ts` (test shape conventions).
- `packages/workspace-git-server/src/client/workspace-id.ts` (just-merged sibling — same comment-block style).

## Before you begin

If you have questions about:
- Whether `shardUrl` should accept `ShardLocator` directly (without `shardIndex`) and return all N URLs vs. one URL — DO NOT change the shape. Use the exact two functions specified.
- The scheme. It's `http://` (not `https://`) — the host pod ↔ storage tier traffic is in-cluster + bearer-auth, no TLS today. Same posture as `@ax/workspace-git-http`.
- Whether to validate `port` (1..65535) — DO NOT validate here. Document in the comment that callers validate at config load.

**Ask if anything else is unclear.** Otherwise proceed.

## Your job

1. Implement Task 3 (failing test). Verify it fails for the right reason (module missing).
2. Commit Task 3.
3. Implement Task 4 (impl).
4. Verify all tests pass (`pnpm --filter @ax/workspace-git-server test` → all green, count UP).
5. Commit Task 4.
6. Self-review.
7. Report back.

## Self-review

- **Completeness:** All 5 test categories present (shardIndex 0, sweep 1-31, shardUrlForWorkspace composition, determinism, shards-count-matters)?
- **Quality:** No `any`, no `validateXxx` boilerplate. Imports use `.js`. Comment explains WHY (DNS shape comes from k8s headless service convention).
- **Discipline:** Only `shardUrl`, `shardUrlForWorkspace`, and `ShardLocator` exported. No CLI, no config helper. No unrelated changes.
- **Testing:** Tests verify behavior — don't just `expect(url).toContain(...)`. Use `expect(url).toBe('http://gs-0.gs.ns.svc.cluster.local:7780')` (full string equality).

## Report format

- What you implemented.
- Test count before → after.
- Files changed.
- Two commit SHAs (`git log --oneline -3`).
- Self-review findings.
- Concerns.

Self-review against the checklist:

**Completeness:**
- shardIndex 0 — explicit test, full string equality ✓
- Sweep 1-31 — `it.each(Array.from({length: 31}, (_, i) => i + 1))` ✓
- shardUrlForWorkspace composition for hand-picked workspaceIds — 4 ids via `it.each` ✓
- Determinism over 100 calls ✓
- Different `shards` count produces different distributions — 8 ids, shards=4 vs shards=7, asserts not-equal ✓

**Quality:**

## Task 4: shard-router impl

**File to create:** `packages/workspace-git-server/src/client/shard-router.ts`

Implementation per the Phase 2 plan:

```ts
import { shardForWorkspace } from '../shared/shard.js';

export interface ShardLocator {
  serviceName: string;
  namespace: string;
  port: number;
  shards: number;
}

export function shardUrl(opts: {
  serviceName: string;
  namespace: string;
  port: number;
  shardIndex: number;
}): string {
  return `http://${opts.serviceName}-${opts.shardIndex}.${opts.serviceName}.${opts.namespace}.svc.cluster.local:${opts.port}`;
}

export function shardUrlForWorkspace(workspaceId: string, opts: ShardLocator): string {
  const shardIndex = shardForWorkspace(workspaceId, opts.shards);
  return shardUrl({ ...opts, shardIndex });
}
```

Add a brief comment block at the top explaining:
- These functions compose Kubernetes-headless-Service per-pod DNS names. The shape `<serviceName>-<N>.<serviceName>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local` is what a `StatefulSet` + headless `Service` produces by default (per-replica stable DNS).
- `shardUrlForWorkspace` is the production hot path: given a workspaceId, return the URL of the storage tier shard that owns it.
- Inputs are NOT validated here. Callers should validate `serviceName` / `namespace` / `port` at config-load time (in `@ax/preset-k8s`), not on every hook call. workspaceId is derived from `workspaceIdFor` upstream and is therefore regex-safe.

Run: `pnpm --filter @ax/workspace-git-server test` → tests pass. Confirm test count went up.

**Commit:** `feat(workspace-git-server): shard-router URL composition`

## Architectural context

This is part of Phase 2 of the workspace redesign (`docs/plans/2026-05-01-workspace-redesign-phase-2-plan.md`). The function `shardUrlForWorkspace` will be the production hot path for the new host plugin to look up "which storage tier shard owns this workspace." Phase 1 already shipped `shardForWorkspace(workspaceId, shards)` returning a numeric shard index (sha256 first 4 bytes mod N) at `packages/workspace-git-server/src/shared/shard.ts`.

The DNS shape comes from how Kubernetes renders a StatefulSet + headless Service: `<sts-name>-<ordinal>.<headless-service-name>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local`. In our chart, the StatefulSet name and the headless Service name are intentionally the same (e.g., `ax-next-git-server`), so callers pass one `serviceName` value.

Key codebase conventions:
- TypeScript strict mode; no `any`.
